title: practical english usage - Part 219

When  the reference is very close to the original mention, repetition normally use a pronoun (e.g. he, she, it) instead of repeating the original noun When  we  refer again to a person or thing that has already been mentioned, we 1  avoiding       unnatural/ungrammatical                 repetition 282 pronouns       and   other     proforms For the use of out-of-date grammar and vocabulary in ceremonies and other situations, ►    318.10.

For discriminatory and offensive language, ►    335.   For slang, ►    334.

For formal and informal ways of using people's names and titles, ►    326.

For structures used in polite requests and questions, ►    310-312.

For a note on formal and informal vocabulary, ►    333 INFORMAL: We think it's possible.

We think that it is possible.

Have you seen Mr Andrews?

Ellipsis (leaving out words, ►    275-280) is more common in informal language.

either no punctuation (if the clauses are short) or a comma  (,) before the When  a conjunction comes between two clauses in writing, there is normally if, that and, but, or, so, before, after, when, as soon as, because, since, although, conjunctions: join clauses into sentences, and show the relationship between them. Some Conjunctions (►  Section 20) make grammatical and meaning connections: they 1  the   difference 283 li nking     wi  th  conjunctions         and   adverbials Your bicycle wheels - why don't we put them in the garden shed?

That friend of your mother's - she's on the phone.

However, this kind of repetition is common in informal speech, when people (More natural than .. . Professor Anson, I spoke to him  yesterday.) Good morning Jack. I spoke to Professor Anson yesterday.

The wall needed painting, (     The-wallH^-needed-pamtmg) normally use a pronoun to repeat a noun phrase that comes in the same clause.
